comments.content:
So the key idea here is checking suspicious emails without risking your actual system. The author nearly lost their email account because they deleted legitimate warnings thinking they were phishing attempts. Their solution is creating a throwaway virtual machine with fake credentials (generic name, simple password) that runs completely isolated from your main computer.

You load the suspicious email or link into this locked-down VM, click away safely, and if it's malicious, nothing on your real Mac gets compromised. You can reuse the VM by duplicating it or just build fresh ones each time. Pretty clever workaround for the catch-22 where legitimate security warnings look identical to phishing attacks.

comments.content:
Looks solid, but I don’t see any mention of PRIMARY selection on Wayland. GNOME still has both CLIPBOARD and PRIMARY; ignoring PRIMARY means middle‑click paste histories won’t be captured, which is what bites most clipboard extensions. Also, your Quick Start suggests Alt+F2 → r, which only restarts Shell on Xorg; on Wayland it just logs you out, so you’ll confuse users during install/upgrade.

One more edge

comments.content:
Looking at the versioning strategy here, I think we'll see more GNOME extensions split their codebases like this within the next year or two. The platform is moving fast enough that maintaining backward compatibility in a single branch becomes genuinely painful, especially once you cross the ESM boundary. ClipFlow's dual zip approach sidesteps the maintenance nightmare but doubles the release overhead.

What's interesting is how this pattern might reshape the extension ecosystem's expectations. Right now users expect an extension to "just work" across multiple GNOME versions, but that's increasingly at odds with the platform's evolution. I wouldn't be surprised if we end up with something like a compatibility layer service or if GNOME eventually forks their extension repository by major version bands. The alternative is developers burn out trying to support four year ranges simultaneously, or they just drop older versions entirely and leave stable distro users stranded. The clipboard manager space is competitive enough that someone will probably fork this for LTS support if the author ever narrows the compatibility window.

comments.content: What could go right here is a quiet but real lift in day-to-day throughput composes into measurable gains for developers, writers, and researchers over weeks. The attention to GNOME 45–50 ESM compliance, async I/O, and avoiding Gtk in the shell process signals a maturing extension that will survive upgrades, which matters more than features alone. Export/import and dedup seed reproducible workflows and team sharing. If widely adopted, this can normalize clipboard curation as a first-class UX pattern across Linux desktops.

comments.content:
What interests me here is how something as mundane as clipboard history keeps reappearing as a third party tool rather than an integrated part of the desktop’s "mental model." GNOME has long prioritized conceptual simplicity, while actual user cognition is cumulative and messy, closer to a stack or graph of fragments that you revisit and recombine. A pinned, starred history effectively becomes a lightweight personal knowledge buffer. The more people rely on this, the more the "true" working surface shifts from files and windows to transient text, and that is a different notion of what a desktop environment is for.

The compatibility and compliance notes are almost more revealing than the feature list. You can read in them a gradual formalization of the extension ecosystem, with constraints on APIs, process boundaries, and module systems, in order to stabilize a layer that has become de facto essential. When a user upgrades from GNOME 44 to 47 and their clipboard habits survive intact, what they experience is not just a well maintained plugin, but a quiet shift toward treating JS extensions as part of the platform contract itself.

comments.content:
If clipboard managers really take off on Linux desktops, we might see a shift in how credential stuffing and phishing attacks work in that ecosystem. Right now most clipboard security tools focus on clearing sensitive data after X seconds, but with persistent history baked into the shell, malware could scrape months of clipboard content in one go. The trade-off is convenience versus a dramatically expanded attack surface, especially for users who copy API keys, passwords, or session tokens before their password manager catches up.

The other question is whether GNOME extension maintainers can keep pace with the shell's release cadence long term. This project already spans eight major versions with two separate codebases for old and new GNOME. When you need build scripts to create "legacy" and "modern" artifacts for software that's only a couple years apart, that's a hint the platform underneath is moving too fast for volunteer maintainers. Eventually you end up with a fragmented ecosystem where half the extensions lag two versions behind or get abandoned entirely.
